subject: ap-government
theme: Civil liberties (protections from government) and civil rights (equal treatment under law) have expanded through the 14th Amendment's incorporation doctrine and landmark Supreme Court rulings that progressively applied Bill of Rights protections against state governments.
core_concepts: Civil liberties vs. civil rights: civil liberties are constitutional protections against government overreach (Bill of Rights); civil rights are legal protections against discrimination based on race, sex, religion, etc. — the 14th Amendment's Equal Protection Clause anchors civil rights; the 1st–8th Amendments anchor civil liberties.

Selective incorporation: the Supreme Court applies Bill of Rights protections to state governments case-by-case through the 14th Amendment's due process clause — most provisions have been incorporated; the 2nd Amendment was incorporated in McDonald v. Chicago (2010).

First Amendment freedoms: speech (Schenck's 'clear and present danger' test; Tinker — symbolic speech protected; NY Times v. Sullivan — actual malice standard), religion (Engel v. Vitale — school prayer violates Establishment Clause; Wisconsin v. Yoder — free exercise can override compulsory education), and press (prior restraint requires extraordinary justification — NY Times Co. v. United States/Pentagon Papers).

Equal protection tiers: strict scrutiny (race, national origin, fundamental rights — government must show compelling interest and narrow tailoring); intermediate scrutiny (sex/gender); rational basis (economic, social regulations — most laws survive). Brown v. Board of Education (1954) applied strict scrutiny to end de jure school segregation.

Civil rights movement and legislation: the Civil Rights Act of 1964 (no discrimination in public accommodations and employment), Voting Rights Act of 1965 (banned literacy tests, preclearance for covered jurisdictions), and 24th Amendment (abolished poll tax) dismantled the legal apparatus of Jim Crow segregation.

Due process rights: 4th Amendment (Mapp v. Ohio — exclusionary rule applied to states), 5th Amendment (Miranda v. Arizona — warnings required before custodial interrogation), 6th Amendment (Gideon v. Wainwright — right to counsel applies to states). Procedural due process ensures fair procedures; substantive due process limits what government may restrict.

Privacy and reproductive rights: the right to privacy is not in the text but was recognized in Griswold v. Connecticut (1965) through penumbras of Bill of Rights provisions; Roe v. Wade (1973) extended privacy to abortion decisions — later modified by Dobbs v. Jackson (2022).

Schenck v. United States (1919): established that speech creating a 'clear and present danger' of harm is not protected — the Holmes test for First Amendment limits in wartime was later refined toward more speech-protective standards in Brandenburg v. Ohio (1969).
exam_tip: AP exam questions on Unit 3 almost always use a SCOTUS case as stimulus — know the 15 required cases by case name, holding, and constitutional provision; be able to explain selective incorporation and identify which level of scrutiny applies to a given classification (race = strict; sex = intermediate; economic = rational basis).
